title: Geometry
objective: Students will explore geometry concepts including Degrees and turns, What Is an Angle?, Measuring angles and more.

steps.content: Today we're going to learn about Degrees and turns. Get ready for an adventure!
Know that angles are measured in degrees, where one degree is 1/360 of a full turn; understand that an angle turning through n one-degree angles has a measure of n degrees
Time to try this out: Explain that a full turn is 360° and a right angle is 90°
If you knows a full circle is 360 degrees, can they tell you how many degrees a quarter turn is — and explain roughly what one single degree looks like?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Degrees and turns: Explain that a full turn is 360° and a right angle is 90° Describe what 'one degree' means in terms of a fraction of a circle
Let's discover the amazing world of What Is an Angle?!
Understand that an angle is a geometric shape formed by two rays sharing a common endpoint (vertex); recognise angles in real-life contexts and 2-D shapes
Let me show you something cool. Identify the vertex and arms of an angle in a diagram
If you draw two lines starting from the same point on paper, can you tell you that the space between them is an angle — and point to the vertex where they meet?
Great job! Today we learned about What Is an Angle?. Identify the vertex and arms of an angle in a diagram Find examples of angles in the classroom (e.g. open door, clock hands)
Let's explore Measuring angles! Have you ever wondered about this?
Measure angles in whole-number degrees using a protractor; draw given angles and sketch angles of specified measure
Let's practice together! Measure an angle with a protractor and read 47°
If you is given a protractor and an angle drawn on paper, can they measure it accurately and then draw a new angle of a size you specify?
Great job! Today we learned about Measuring angles. Measure an angle with a protractor and read 47° Draw an angle of 135° using a protractor
Have you ever thought about Lines, Rays & Angles? Let's find out together!
Draw and identify points, lines, line segments, rays, angles (right, acute, obtuse), and perpendicular and parallel lines; identify these in two-dimensional figures
Let me show you something cool. Draw a pair of perpendicular lines and a pair of parallel lines
If you is given a piece of paper and a ruler, can they draw a line segment, mark a point, and then draw another line through that point that runs perfectly parallel to the first?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Lines, Rays & Angles: Draw a pair of perpendicular lines and a pair of parallel lines Identify all pairs of parallel sides in a trapezoid
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Angle Sum Rules!
Know that angles at a point sum to 360° (one whole turn), angles on a straight line sum to 180°, and vertically opposite angles are equal; use these facts to find missing angles
Time to try this out: State that two angles on a straight line add to 180°
If three angles meet at a point and two of them are 120° and 95°, can you work out the third angle — knowing all the angles at a point add up to 360°?
Great job! Today we learned about Angle Sum Rules. State that two angles on a straight line add to 180° Explain that four right angles at a point make 360°
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Regular and irregular polygons!
Distinguish between regular and irregular polygons based on reasoning about equal sides and equal angles
Let's practice together! Classify a set of polygons as regular or irregular
If you show you a stop sign (regular octagon) and a wonky eight-sided shape, can they explain which is regular and which is irregular — and why?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Regular and irregular polygons: Classify a set of polygons as regular or irregular Explain that a regular pentagon has 5 equal sides and 5 equal angles
Let's discover the amazing world of Measuring angles (age 9+)!
Recognise angle measure as additive; find unknown angles by adding or subtracting on a diagram using equations with a symbol for the unknown
Let's practice together! Two angles on a straight line are 65° and x°; find x = 115°
If two angles sit next to each other on a straight line and one is 65°, can you work out the other angle — knowing that angles on a straight line always add up to 180°?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Measuring angles (age 9+). Two angles on a straight line are 65° and x°; find x = 115° An angle is decomposed into 35° and 40°; state the whole angle is 75°
Let's discover the amazing world of Estimating Angles!
Estimate and compare acute, obtuse, and reflex angles in degrees; classify angles by type and order them by size
Time to try this out: Estimate an angle as approximately 130° and classify it as obtuse
If you show you three different angles drawn on paper, can they estimate which is biggest, label each as acute, obtuse, or reflex — and then check with a protractor?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Estimating Angles. Estimate an angle as approximately 130° and classify it as obtuse Identify a reflex angle in a diagram and estimate it as about 270°
Let's discover the amazing world of Classifying shapes by line properties!
Classify two-dimensional figures based on the presence or absence of parallel or perpendicular lines, or angles of a specified size; recognise right triangles as a category
Let's practice together! Sort a set of quadrilaterals into those with parallel sides and those without

If you show you a mixed set of triangles and quadrilaterals, can they sort them into groups — like "shapes with at least one right angle" or "shapes with parallel sides" — and explain their reasoning?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Classifying shapes by line properties. Sort a set of quadrilaterals into those with parallel sides and those without Identify which triangles in a set are right triangles
Have you ever thought about Understanding angles (age 9+)? Let's find out together!
Use the properties of rectangles to deduce related facts and find missing lengths and angles
Let me show you something cool. Given one side of a rectangle is 8 cm and the perimeter is 28 cm, find the other side
If you knows one side of a rectangular garden is 7 m, can they tell you the length of the opposite side and all four corner angles — without measuring?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Understanding angles (age 9+): Given one side of a rectangle is 8 cm and the perimeter is 28 cm, find the other side Explain that all angles in a rectangle are 90°
Let's discover the amazing world of 3-D shapes (age 9+)!
Identify 3-D shapes, including cubes and other cuboids, from 2-D representations
Let me show you something cool. Identify a cuboid from its net
If you show you a flat architect's sketch of a box or a pyramid, can they tell you the name of the 3-D shape it represents?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about 3-D shapes (age 9+). Identify a cuboid from its net Name the 3-D shape shown in an isometric drawing
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Transformations on a Grid!
Identify, describe, and represent the position of a shape following a reflection or translation using appropriate language; know that the shape has not changed
Time to try this out: Reflect a triangle in a vertical mirror line on a coordinate grid and state the new coordinates
If you slides or flips a shape on squared paper, can they describe exactly where it ends up and confirm the shape itself hasn't changed size or angles?

Great job! Today we learned about Transformations on a Grid. Reflect a triangle in a vertical mirror line on a coordinate grid and state the new coordinates Translate a shape 3 units right and 2 units up and describe the movement
